title: MCP as Governed Capability Protocol
context: The Model Context Protocol (MCP), introduced by Anthropic in November 2024 and donated to the Agentic AI Foundation under the Linux Foundation in December 2025, is the de facto standard for connecting LLMs to external tools, resources, and data sources. As of June 2026, there are over 10,000 active public MCP servers with 97+ million monthly SDK downloads and 28% of Fortune 500 companies deploying MCP servers for production AI workflows. Major development environments (VS Code Copilot, Cursor v0.43+, JetBrains 2025.2+) have integrated MCP support. Major AI providers (OpenAI, Google Gemini, Microsoft) added native MCP support in early 2026. The TypeScript SDK v2 stable release is anticipated Q3 2026 alongside an updated specification (2026-07-28 RC). MCP defines four primitives: tools (actions with side effects), resources (data sources for loading into context), prompts (reusable templates with parameters), and Tasks (SEP-1686 durable async execution with state machines). Two transports: stdio (local subprocess, pipes) and Streamable HTTP (remote, single-endpoint, SSE streaming — replacing legacy HTTP+SSE). Authorization is built on OAuth 2.1 with PKCE, per-tool token scoping (mcp:tool: prefix), short-lived tokens with refresh rotation, and resource indicators per RFC 8707. MCP is the plug shape. It is not the governance layer. Tribunus must layer capability governance — policy classification, risk assessment, evidence contracts, and receipt recording — on top of MCP's protocol.
decision:
### MCP Spec 2025-11-25 and July 2026 Release Candidate
Latest stable specification is 2025-11-25, which introduced authorization server discovery, icon metadata for tools, incremental scope consent, and experimental Task support. A 2026-07-28 specification release candidate is under development (tagged on GitHub), expected to ship alongside TypeScript SDK v2 stable in Q3 2026.

Confidence: high

### MCP Primitives — Tools, Resources, Prompts, and Tasks (SEP-1686)
Core primitives: Tools (functions AI models execute), Resources (context/data exposed to models), Prompts (templated messages and workflows). The MCP Tasks API (SEP-1686, formalized in 2025-11-25 spec) enables durable async execution — a 'call-now, fetch-later' pattern for operations exceeding network timeouts. Tasks API defines a standardized state machine (created → working → completed/failed/cancelled), polling endpoints (tasks/get, tasks/list, tasks/cancel, tasks/result), and capability negotiation via Initialize. Task-augmented requests degrade gracefully with non-Tasks servers via synchronous inline fallback.

Confidence: high

### Streamable HTTP Transport — Single Endpoint, SSE Inside
Streamable HTTP is the standard remote HTTP transport as of 2025-03-26 spec; the old HTTP+SSE (two-endpoint) transport is deprecated/legacy from 2024-11-05. Streamable HTTP exposes a single endpoint (e.g., /mcp). Client POSTs JSON-RPC with Accept: application/json, text/event-stream. Server can return JSON or text/event-stream. SSE framing is still allowed inside Streamable HTTP — what's deprecated is the old two-endpoint model (GET /sse + POST /messages), not SSE itself. Headers: MCP-Protocol-Version, MCP-Session-Id for session management. stdio transport remains standard for local subprocess integrations. Atlassian Rovo deprecated legacy SSE endpoint in favor of Streamable HTTP, citing reliability and spec alignment.

Confidence: high

### MCP OAuth 2.1 with PKCE — Authorization Standard
MCP specification mandates OAuth 2.1 with PKCE as the authorization standard (added June 2025). PKCE mandatory for all clients including confidential clients; S256 challenge required. Token scoping per-tool (e.g., mcp:tool:read_file:read). Resource indicators (RFC 8707) bind tokens to specific MCP servers. Servers must validate aud claim in access tokens. Metadata discovery mandated via OAuth 2.0 Authorization Server Metadata (RFC 8414) and Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728). Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591) strongly recommended. Short-lived access tokens (15-60 min) with refresh token rotation. Strict redirect URI validation (exact string match, no wildcards). OAuth 2.1 removes Implicit Grant and ROPC. Error handling requires specific HTTP status codes (401 unauthorized, 403 insufficient scopes) with WWW-Authenticate headers.

Confidence: high

### MCP Gateway/Proxy Patterns — Enterprise Managed Auth and SSO
MCP gateways serve as critical control plane between AI agents and tools — centralizing authentication, audit trails, and real-time monitoring. Triple-gate architecture emerging: (1) AI Gateway validates natural language instructions, (2) MCP/Tool Gateway controls tool access with fine-grained authorization, (3) API Gateway protects backend services. Enterprise SSO integration via SAML 2.0, OpenID Connect (OIDC), supporting Okta, Microsoft Entra ID, Google Workspace. Gateways drive authorization decisions from JWT claims. Policy enforcement includes RBAC, Fine-Grained Authorization (FGA), and Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC). Policy-as-Code managed in version control for auditability. Zero Trust principles applied — no entity trusted by default.

Confidence: high

### MCP Ecosystem Scale — 10,000+ Servers, 97M+ Monthly Downloads
Over 10,000 active public MCP servers as of April/May 2026. Official registry records 9,652 latest server records as of May 24, 2026. 97+ million monthly SDK downloads by April 2026. GitHub: 15,926 repos with mcp-server topic. 28% of Fortune 500 companies have deployed MCP servers for production AI workflows. Major IDE support: Cursor IDE v0.43+, VS Code via .vscode/mcp.json, JetBrains MCP Server plugin built-in since 2025.2. Major AI providers added native MCP support: OpenAI, Google (Gemini), Microsoft in early 2026. Community-built servers for GitHub, Slack, PostgreSQL, Stripe, and thousands more.

Confidence: high

### MCP Donation to AAIF (Linux Foundation)
December 9, 2025: Anthropic donated MCP to the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), a directed fund under the Linux Foundation. Co-founded by Anthropic, Block, and OpenAI. Founding projects: Anthropic's MCP, Block's 'goose' agent framework, OpenAI's 'AGENTS.md' specification. Platinum-level support from Google, Microsoft, AWS, Cloudflare, Bloomberg. AAIF grew to 170+ member organizations by April 2026. The Linux Foundation provides neutral governance similar to its stewardship of Kubernetes and PyTorch. Linux Foundation also launched Tokenomics Foundation (June 3, 2026) for AI infrastructure economics.

Confidence: high

### Capability-Based Security Models — UCAN, ZCAP, Object-Capability
UCAN (User-Controlled Authorization Network): distributed, trustless, delegable authorization scheme. Publicly verifiable via late-bound certificate chains + DIDs. Supports caveats (policy restrictions). ZCAP-LD (Linked Data Capabilities): represents capabilities within the Object-Capability (OCap) model, serialized as IPLD objects. OCap model characteristics: unforgeability, transferability, least privilege, elimination of ambient authority (prevents privilege escalation). UCAN and ZCAP underpin agent security alignment: granular, unforgeable rights for agents to specific actions/resources — limiting harm even if compromised. CoSAI (Coalition for Secure AI) released January 2026 whitepaper identifying 12 core threat categories for MCP, including improper authentication and missing access control.

Confidence: high
consequences:
### Positive
- Interoperability without governance leakage: MCP provides the plug; Tribunus provides the circuit breaker, ledger, and court record.
- 10,000+ existing servers become potential capabilities — filesystem, GitHub, Slack, PostgreSQL, web search, and more can be governed through Tribunus.
- Gateway positioning: "Tribunus normalizes tool chaos" is a stronger product story than "we also do MCP."
- Long-term value in governance, not integration: when everyone supports MCP, the differentiator is who governs it best.
- The AAIF/Linux Foundation stewardship (170+ member organizations, platinum support from Google/Microsoft/AWS/Cloudflare/Bloomberg) ensures neutral governance and long-term stability.
- MCP's OAuth 2.1 with PKCE and per-tool scoping provides a solid foundation for Tribunus' action-level authorization pipeline.

### Negative
- MCP is still evolving: the July 2026 specification release is a major update. Tribunus should couple loosely — the MCP adapter is replaceable if the spec changes.
- Action-level authorization is recognized as crucial but not yet fully standardized across all MCP implementations. Tribunus' classification pipeline is additive.
- Gateway adds an abstraction layer between the raw MCP ecosystem and Tribunus-governed capabilities.
- MCP's Tasks API (SEP-1686) for durable async execution is still experimental — state machine semantics may shift before stable release in Q3 2026.
- Capability-based security models (UCAN, ZCAP, OCap) are promising for agent security alignment but have limited MCP ecosystem adoption as of mid-2026; Tribunus may need to pioneer their integration.
- EU AI Act high-risk requirements (effective August 2026) and emerging agent governance frameworks (CoSAI threat taxonomy, NIST AI RMF) add compliance pressure that may accelerate API changes.
